title: Mumbai Egg Bhurji Pav

subs.name: toasted whole wheat bread slices or a folded roti work if pav rolls are unavailable
ghee gives a slightly richer, nuttier flavour in the same quantity; or use oil for a lighter bhurji
a pinch of curry powder or pav bhaji masala gives the bhurji a Mumbai street-stall character

steps.text: Crack all 8 eggs into a bowl, add a pinch of salt and turmeric, and whisk briefly until the yolks and whites are just combined — bhurji is made with lightly beaten eggs, not a uniformly smooth mixture. A few streaks of white are fine.

Heat 2 tablespoons of butter on a tawa or wide frying pan over medium-high heat. Add cumin seeds and let them splutter for 10 seconds. Add garlic (if using) and ginger and stir for 20 seconds. Add green chilli and onion and cook, stirring, for 3–4 minutes until the onion turns soft and golden at the edges.

Add the chopped tomato and cook, pressing with the spatula, for 3 minutes until the tomato breaks down and the mixture thickens. Add red chilli powder, garam masala, and salt. Stir for 30 seconds until the spices are absorbed and the masala looks slightly glossy.

Pour the beaten eggs over the masala in one go. Reduce heat to medium. Using a flat spatula or wooden spoon, fold and stir the eggs continuously in large, slow sweeps — the goal is large, soft, pillowy curds, not fine scrambled egg. The eggs are done when they are just set but still look slightly glossy and moist, about 2–3 minutes. Do not cook until dry. Remove from heat immediately — residual heat will continue to cook them. Fold in coriander leaves.

While the bhurji rests for a minute, halve the pav rolls, spread a little butter on the cut sides, and toast them cut-side down on the same tawa over medium heat for 60–90 seconds until lightly golden and crisp. Serve the egg bhurji in a deep plate with the toasted pav alongside for scooping. Street vendors traditionally also crack a raw egg on the tawa as a finish topper — add a fried egg on top of the bhurji for the full experience.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: butter → ghee gives a slightly richer, nuttier flavour in the same quantity; or use oil for a lighter bhurji; bread → toasted whole wheat bread slices or a folded roti work if pav rolls are unavailable; garam-masala → a pinch of curry powder or pav bhaji masala gives the bhurji a Mumbai street-stall character
